input.background: Suppose a screening test has a sensitivity of 0.98 and a false-positive rate of 0.01. The test is used in a population that has a disease prevalence of 0.001.
input.question: Determine the predictive value of a positive test under these circumstances.

output.method: 事件发生概率及独立性
output.answer:
We can use Bayes' theorem to determine the predictive value of a positive test under these circumstances as follows

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Pr}(D \mid T) & =\frac{\text { sensitivity × prevalence }}{[\text { sensitivity × prevalence }]+[\text { false positive rate } \times(1-\text { prevalence })]} \
& =\frac{0.98 \times 0.001}{[0.98 \times 0.001]+[0.01 \times(1-0.001)]}=0.0893
\end{aligned}
$$
The probability of having disease given a positive test result is $8.93 \%$.

sensitivity.description: The probability that the test correctly identifies a positive case when the disease is present.

false_positive_rate.description: The probability that the test incorrectly identifies a positive case when the disease is not present.

prevalence.description: The proportion of the population that has the disease.

predictive_value_positive.description: The probability that a person actually has the disease given that they tested positive.

meta_info.caption: Predictive value positive.
meta_info.section: 5.5  MORE RULES AND PROPERTIES OF PROBABILITY
